volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Hippolytus (Section 1085 - Section 1275)

sections.label: Section 1085
sections.original: πάλαι ξενοῦσθαι τόνδε προυννέποντά με ;
sections.translation: my proclamation long ago condemning him to exile .

sections.label: Section 1086
sections.original: κλαίων τις αὐτῶν ἆρ ’ ἐμοῦ τεθίξεται ·
sections.translation: Whoso of them doth lay a hand on me shall rue it ; thyself expel me , if thy spirit move thee , from the land .

sections.label: Section 1088
sections.original: δράσω τάδ ’, εἰ μὴ τοῖς ἐμοῖς πείσῃ λόγοις ·
sections.translation: I will , unless my word thou straight obey ; no pity for thy exile steals into my heart . [ Exit Theseus .

sections.label: Section 1090
sections.original: ἄραρεν , ὡς ἔοικεν · ὦ τάλας ἐγώ ·
sections.translation: The sentence then , it seems , is passed . Ah , misery I How well I know the truth herein , but know no way to tell it ! O daughter of Latona , dearest to me of all deities , partner , comrade in the chase , far from glorious Athens must I fly . Farewell , city

sections.label: Section 1095
sections.original: καὶ γαῖ ’ Ἐρεχθέως · ὦ πέδον Τροζήνιον ,
sections.translation: and land of Erechtheus ; farewell , Troezen , most joyous home wherein to pass the spring of life ; ’ tis my last sight of thee , farewell !

sections.label: Section 1098
sections.original: ἴτ ’ ὦ νέοι μοι τῆσδε γῆς ὁμήλικες ,
sections.translation: Come , my comrades in this land , young like me , greet me kindly and escort me forth ,

sections.label: Section 1100
sections.original: ὡς οὔποτ ’ ἄλλον ἄνδρα σωφρονέστερον
sections.translation: for never will ye behold a purer soul , for all my father ’ s doubts . [ Exit Hippolytus .

sections.label: Section 1.1102
sections.original: ἦ μέγα μοι τὰ θεῶν μελεδήμαθ ’, ὅταν φρένας ἔλθῃ ,
sections.translation: In very deed the thoughts I have about the gods , whenso they come into my mind , do much to soothe its grief , but though I cherish secret hopes of some great guiding will , yet am I at fault when I survey the fate and doings of the sons of men ;

sections.label: Section 1.1105
sections.original: ἄλλα γὰρ ἄλλοθεν ἀμείβεται , μετὰ δ ’ ἵσταται ἀνδράσιν
sections.translation: change succeeds to change , and man ’ s life veers and shifts in endless restlessness .

sections.label: Section 1.1111
sections.original: εἴθε μοι εὐξαμένᾳ θεόθεν τάδε μοῖρα παράσχοι ,
sections.translation: Fortune grant me this , I pray , at heaven ’ s hand ,— a happy lot in life and a soul from sorrow free ;

sections.label: Section 1.1115
sections.original: δόξα δὲ μήτ ’ ἀτρεκὴς μήτ ’ αὖ παράσημος ἐνείη ·
sections.translation: opinions let me hold not too precise nor yet too hollow ; but , lightly changing my habits to each morrow as it comes , may I thus attain a life of bliss !

sections.label: Section 2.1120
sections.original: οὐκέτι γὰρ καθαρὰν φρέν ’ ἔχω τὰ παρ ’ ἐλπίδα λεύσ-
sections.translation: For now no more is my mind free from doubts , unlooked-for sights greet my vision ; for lo ! I see the morning star of Athens , eye of Hellas , driven by his father ’ s fury

sections.label: Section 2.1125
sections.original: ἄλλαν ἐπ ’ αἶαν ἱέμενον .
sections.translation: to another land . Mourn , ye sands of my native shores , ye oak-groves on the hills , where with his fleet hounds he would hunt the quarry to the death ,

sections.label: Section 2.1130
sections.original: Δίκτυνναν ἀμφὶ σεμνάν .
sections.translation: attending on Dictynna , awful queen .

sections.label: Section 2.1131
sections.original: οὐκέτι συζυγίαν πώλων Ἐνετᾶν ἐπιβάσῃ
sections.translation: No more will he mount his car drawn by Venetian steeds , filling the course round Limna with the prancing [*] of his trained horses . Nevermore in his fathers house shall he wake the Muse

sections.label: Section 2.1135
sections.original: λήξει πατρῷον ἀνὰ δόμον ·
sections.translation: that never slept beneath his lute-strings ; no hand will crown the spots where rests the maiden Latona ’ mid the boskage deep ; nor evermore shall our virgins vie to win thy love , now thou art banished ;

sections.label: Section 1142
sections.original: — ἐγὼ δὲ σᾷ δυστυχίᾳ
sections.translation: while I with tears at thy unhappy fate shall endure a lot all undeserved . Ah ! hapless mother ,

sections.label: Section 1145
sections.original: τερ , ἔτεκες ἀνόνατα · φεῦ ·
sections.translation: in vain didst thou bring forth , it seems . I am angered with the gods ; out upon them ! O ye linked Graces , why are ye sending from his native land this poor youth , a guiltless sufferer ,

sections.label: Section 1150
sections.original: πέμπετε τῶνδ ’ ἀπ ’ οἴκων ;
sections.translation: far from his home ?

sections.label: Section 1151
sections.original: — καὶ μὴν ὀπαδὸν Ἱππολύτου τόνδ ’ εἰσορῶ
sections.translation: But lo ! I see a servant of Hippolytus hasting with troubled looks towards the palace .

sections.label: Section 1153
sections.original: ποῖ γῆς ἄνακτα τῆσδε Θησέα μολὼν
sections.translation: Ladies , [*] where may I find Theseus , king of the country ? pray , tell me if

sections.label: Section 1155
sections.original: σημήνατ ’· ἆρα τῶνδε δωμάτων ἔσω ;
sections.translation: ye know ; is he within the palace here ?

sections.label: Section 1156
sections.original: ὅδ ’ αὐτὸς ἔξω δωμάτων πορεύεται .
sections.translation: Lo ! himself approaches from the palace .

sections.label: Section 1157
sections.original: Θησεῦ , μερίμνης ἄξιον φέρω λόγον
sections.translation: Theseus , I am the bearer of troublous tidings to thee and all citizens who dwell in Athens or the bounds of Troezen .

sections.label: Section 1160
sections.original: τί δ ’ ἔστι ; μῶν τις συμφορὰ νεωτέρα
sections.translation: How now ? hath some strange calamity overtaken these two neighbouring cities ?

sections.label: Section 1162
sections.original: Ἱππόλυτος οὐκέτ ’ ἔστιν , ὡς εἰπεῖν ἔπος ·
sections.translation: In one brief word , Hippolytus is dead . ’ Tis true one slender thread still links him to the light of life .

sections.label: Section 1164
sections.original: πρὸς τοῦ ; δι ’ ἔχθρας μῶν τις ἦν ἀφιγμένος ,
sections.translation: Who slew him ? Did some husband come to blows with him ,

sections.label: Section 1165
sections.original: ὅτου κατῄσχυν ’ ἄλοχον ὡς πατρὸς βίᾳ ;
sections.translation: one whose wife , like mine , had suffered brutal violence ?

sections.label: Section 1166
sections.original: οἰκεῖος αὐτὸν ὤλεσ ’ ἁρμάτων ὄχος
sections.translation: He perished through those steeds that drew his chariot , and through the curses thou didst utter , praying to thy sire , the ocean-king , to slay thy son .

sections.label: Section 1169
sections.original: ὦ θεοὶ Πόσειδόν θ ’, ὡς ἄρ ’ ἦσθ ’ ἐμὸς πατὴρ
sections.translation: Ye gods and king Poseidon , thou hast proved my parentage

sections.label: Section 1170
sections.original: ὀρθῶς , ἀκούσας τῶν ἐμῶν κατευγμάτων .
sections.translation: by hearkening to my prayer ! Say how he perished ; how fell the uplifted hand of Justice to smite the villain who dishonoured me ?

sections.label: Section 1173
sections.original: ἡμεῖς μὲν ἀκτῆς κυμοδέγμονος πέλας
sections.translation: Hard by the wave-beat shore were we combing out his horses ’ manes ,

sections.label: Section 1175
sections.original: κλαίοντες · ἦλθε γάρ τις ἄγγελος λέγων
sections.translation: weeping the while , for one had come to say that Hippolytus was harshly exiled by thee and nevermore would return to set foot in this land . Then came he , telling the same doleful tale to us upon the beach , and with him was a countless throng

sections.label: Section 1180
sections.original: φίλων ἅμ ’ ἔστειχ ’ ἡλίκων θ ’ ὁμήγυρις .
sections.translation: of friends who followed after . At length he stayed his lamentation and spake : Why weakly rave on this wise ? My father ’ s commands must be obeyed . Ho ! servants , harness my horses to the chariot ; this is no longer now city of mine .

sections.label: Section 1185
sections.original: τοὐνθένδε μέντοι πᾶς ἀνὴρ ἠπείγετο ,
sections.translation: Thereupon each one of us bestirred himself , and , ere a man could say ’ twas done , we had the horses standing ready at our master ’ s side . Then he caught up the reins from the chariot-rail , first fitting his feet [*] exactly in the hollows made for them .

sections.label: Section 1190
sections.original: καὶ πρῶτα μὲν θεοῖς εἶπ ’ ἀναπτύξας χέρας ·
sections.translation: But first with outspread palms he called upon the gods , O Zeus , now strike me dead , if I have sinned , and let my father learn how he is wronging me , in death at least , if not in life .

sections.label: Section 1194
sections.original: κἀν τῷδ ’ ἐπῆγε κέντρον ἐς χεῖρας λαβὼν
sections.translation: Therewith he seized the whip and lashed

sections.label: Section 1195
sections.original: πώλοις ὁμαρτῇ · πρόσπολοι δ ’ ὑφ ’ ἅρματος
sections.translation: each horse in turn ; while we , close by his chariot , near the reins , kept up with him along the road that leads direct to Argos and Epidaurus .

sections.label: Section 1198
sections.original: ἐπεὶ δ ’ ἔρημον χῶρον εἰσεβάλλομεν ,
sections.translation: And just as we were coming to a desert spot , a strip of sand beyond the borders of this country ,

sections.label: Section 1200
sections.original: πρὸς πόντον ἤδη κειμένη Σαρωνικόν .
sections.translation: sloping right to the Saronic gulf , there issued thence a deep rumbling sound , as it were an earthquake , a fearsome noise , and the horses reared their heads and pricked their ears , while we were filled with wild alarm

sections.label: Section 1205
sections.original: πόθεν ποτ ’ εἴη φθόγγος . ἐς δ ’ ἁλιρρόθους
sections.translation: to know whence came the sound ; when , as we gazed toward the wave-beat shore , a wave tremendous we beheld towering to the skies , so that from our view the cliffs of Scirori vanished , for it hid the isthmus and the rock of Asclepius ;

sections.label: Section 1210
sections.original: κἄπειτ ’ ἀνοιδῆσάν τε καὶ πέριξ ἀφρὸν
sections.translation: then swelling and frothing with a crest of foam , the sea discharged it toward the beach where stood the harnessed car , and in the moment that it broke , that mighty wall of waters , there issued from the wave a monstrous bull ,

sections.label: Section 1215
sections.original: οὗ πᾶσα μὲν χθὼν φθέγματος πληρουμένη
sections.translation: whose bellowing filled the land with fearsome echoes , a sight too awful as it seemed to us who witnessed it . A panic seized the horses there and then , but our master , to horses ’ ways quite used ,

sections.label: Section 1220
sections.original: πολὺς ξυνοικῶν ἥρπασ ’ ἡνίας χεροῖν ,
sections.translation: gripped in both hands his reins , and tying them to his body pulled them backward as the sailor pulls his oar ; but the horses gnashed the forged bits between their teeth and bore him wildly on , regardless of their master ’ s guiding hand

sections.label: Section 1225
sections.original: οὔθ ’ ἱπποδέσμων οὔτε κολλητῶν ὄχων
sections.translation: or rein or jointed car . And oft as he would take the guiding rein and steer for softer ground , showed that bull in front to turn him back again , maddening his team with terror ;

sections.label: Section 1230
sections.original: εἰ δ ’ ἐς πέτρας φέροιντο μαργῶσαι φρένας ,
sections.translation: but if in their frantic career they ran towards the rocks , he would draw nigh the chariot-rail , keeping up with them , until , suddenly dashing the wheel against a stone , he upset and wrecked the car ; then was dire confusion ,

sections.label: Section 1235
sections.original: τροχῶν ἐπήδων ἀξόνων τ ’ ἐνήλατα .
sections.translation: axle-boxes and linchpins springing into the air . While he , poor youth , entangled in the reins was dragged along , bound by a stubborn knot , his poor head dashed against the rocks , his flesh all torn , the while he cried out piteously ,

sections.label: Section 1240
sections.original: Στῆτ ’, ὦ φάτναισι ταῖς ἐμαῖς τεθραμμέναι ,
sections.translation: Stay , stay , my horses whom my own hand hath fed at the manger , destroy me not utterly . O luckless curse of a father ! Will no one come and save me for all my virtue ?

sections.label: Section 1243
sections.original: πολλοὶ δὲ βουληθέντες ὑστέρῳ ποδὶ
sections.translation: Now we , though much we longed to help , were left far behind . At last , I know not how , he broke loose

sections.label: Section 1245
sections.original: τμητῶν ἱμάντων οὐ κάτοιδ ’ ὅτῳ τρόπῳ
sections.translation: from the shapely reins that bound him , a faint breath of life still in him ; but the horses disappeared , and that portentous bull , among the rocky ground , I know not where .

sections.label: Section 1249
sections.original: δοῦλος μὲν οὖν ἔγωγε σῶν δόμων , ἄναξ ,
sections.translation: I am but a slave in thy house , ’ tis true , O king ,

sections.label: Section 1250
sections.original: ἀτὰρ τοσοῦτόν γ ’ οὐ δυνήσομαί ποτε ,
sections.translation: yet will I never believe so monstrous a charge against thy son ’ s character , no ! not though the whole race of womankind should hang itself , or one should fill with writing every pine-tree tablet grown on Ida , sure as I am of his uprightness .

sections.label: Section 1255
sections.original: αἰαῖ · κέκρανται συμφορὰ νέων κακῶν ,
sections.translation: Alas ! new troubles come to plague us , nor is there any escape from fate and necessity .

sections.label: Section 1257
sections.original: μίσει μὲν ἀνδρὸς τοῦ πεπονθότος τάδε
sections.translation: My hatred for him who hath thus suffered made me glad at thy tidings , yet from regard for the gods and him , because he is my son ,

sections.label: Section 1260
sections.original: οὔθ ’ ἥδομαι τοῖσδ ’ οὔτ ’ ἐπάχθομαι κακοῖς .
sections.translation: I feel neither joy nor sorrow at his sufferings .

sections.label: Section 1261
sections.original: πῶς οὖν ; κομίζειν , ἢ τί χρὴ τὸν ἄθλιον
sections.translation: But say , are we to bring the victim hither , or how are we to fulfil thy wishes ? Bethink thee ; if by me thou wilt be schooled , thou wilt not harshly treat thy son in his sad plight .

sections.label: Section 1265
sections.original: κομίζετ ’ αὐτόν , ὡς ἰδὼν ἐν ὄμμασιν
sections.translation: Bring him hither , that when I see him face to face , who hath denied having polluted my wife ’ s honour , I may by words and heaven ’ s visitation convict him .

sections.label: Section 1268
sections.original: σὺ τὰν θεῶν ἄκαμπτον φρένα καὶ βροτῶν
sections.translation: Ah ! Cypris , thine the hand that guides the stubborn hearts of gods and men ; thine ,

sections.label: Section 1270
sections.original: ὁ ποικιλόπτερος ἀμφιβαλὼν
sections.translation: and that attendant boy ’ s , who , with painted plumage gay , flutters round his victims on lightning wing . O ’ er the land and booming deep on golden pinion borne flits the god of Love ,

sections.label: Section 1275
sections.original: πτανὸς ἐφορμάσῃ χρυσοφαής ,
sections.translation: maddening the heart and beguiling the senses of all whom he attacks , savage whelps on mountains bred , ocean ’ s monsters , creatures of this sun-warmed earth ,
